Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd just end up scrolling through the LMS without really accomplishing much. I spent about 21 hours a week supposedly studying, but a lot of that was me staring at screens feeling overwhelmed. I'm a visual learner, so I'd watch videos and look at diagrams, but actually sitting down to complete assignments felt like climbing a mountain I didn't have the energy for.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on my anxiety levels that week. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, logging in daily, watching everything. Other weeks I could barely bring myself to open my laptop. Deadlines would create these panic spirals where I'd suddenly be very active but not actually productive. The regularity really depended on how much emotional bandwidth I had left after dealing with everything else in my life.
Q3: Usually it was this nagging guilt that would make me log in. I'd feel bad about not checking in, so I'd open the LMS just to see what I was missing. Sometimes it was notifications about new content or seeing my peers post in discussions. Other times I logged in just to feel like I was doing something, even if I wasn't really engaging with the material meaningfully.
Q4: There was a brief period where checking the LMS became almost compulsive for me. I'd wake up and immediately check it, sometimes multiple times a day. It became routine because my anxiety made me feel like I was constantly falling behind, so I kept checking obsessively. But that routine was more about managing my anxiety than actually learning anything substantial.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I'm such a visual learner. I watched over 94 minutes of content because seeing concepts explained visually helped things click in ways reading never did. The discussion forums were also helpful when my peer support group was active there. Having that social connection made the material feel less isolating and gave me different perspectives on confusing topics.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the assignments, which is embarrassing to admit. My completion rate was terrible because every time I'd look at an assignment, this wave of anxiety would hit me and I'd convince myself I'd do it later. I also avoided the readings that were just walls of text. My brain just couldn't process them the way it could process visual content, so they sat there untouched.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it connected to something I actually cared about. Watching a video felt productive because I could literally see myself making progress. Discussions felt worthwhile when people actually responded and I felt less alone. But anything that required sustained effort with delayed payoff just felt like too much when I was already struggling.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Required ones came with deadlines and consequences, which sometimes forced me to act despite my anxiety. Optional ones just sat there making me feel guilty for not doing them. I'd tell myself I'd get to them eventually, but without external pressure, my motivation just couldn't overcome the inertia of my mental state.
Q9: Yes, I downloaded a lot of materials to study offline. I'd save PDFs and screenshots of diagrams to my tablet and review them away from my computer. Sometimes being on the LMS itself stressed me out because I could see all my incomplete tasks, so studying offline felt safer. I also used YouTube videos that explained concepts differently than our course materials.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my downloaded notes or rewatching saved videos without being logged in. I'd also spend time just thinking about concepts while doing other things, like processing what I'd learned during walks. My peer support group would sometimes meet in person or text about course material, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF, watch or read it once online, then return to it multiple times offline. My tablet was full of course materials that I'd review repeatedly. The LMS would only show that single initial access, but I probably engaged with some materials five or six times offline while trying to understand them.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where everything felt impossible. When my anxiety spiked, even simple tasks felt overwhelming. Around midterms I hit a wall where the accumulated stress of incomplete assignments made me want to avoid the course entirely. That's when the peer support intervention really helped because having people check in on me kept me from completely disappearing.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just... drift. I'd click around without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities made it hard to give the course my full attention. Sometimes I had to choose between keeping up with course videos or actually attempting assignments, and the videos always won because they felt more manageable.
Q14: So many times. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things. I'd read discussion posts without really processing them. Sometimes I'd spend an hour on the LMS and realize I couldn't remember anything I'd looked at. My body was there but my mind was drowning in anxiety.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety around them was so intense that avoidance felt like the only way to cope in the moment. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first, or that I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better. Tomorrow never came the way I hoped it would.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense bursts of panic-driven activity. I'd suddenly be very present on the LMS in the days before something was due, watching everything, reading everything. But the quality of that engagement was poor because I was in survival mode. After deadlines passed, I'd often crash and disappear for a while to recover emotionally.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Good feedback lifted my mood temporarily and made me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even constructive stuff, would send me into a spiral. I'd avoid checking grades or comments sometimes because I was afraid of what I'd see. The peer support helped me process feedback without catastrophizing.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with eight online courses to manage. Having so much content available was supposed to be helpful, but it just made me feel behind all the time. The weekly modules gave some structure, but I often fell behind and then the backlog became its own source of stress. I needed more scaffolding than the course provided.
Q19: The visual elements made engagement easier because that's how I learn best. But the sheer volume of content made it harder. I wished there were clearer priorities about what was essential versus supplementary. The discussion forums helped when they were active, but sometimes they felt like another obligation rather than a support.
Q20: That's literally me. I was constantly active, logging in 47 times, watching videos, checking discussions. But my assignment completion was abysmal. I think it's about the difference between consuming content and producing work. Consuming feels safer, less vulnerable. Submitting work means being judged, and when you're already anxious, that feels unbearable.
Q21: I can see how that would happen. If someone is efficient and studies offline, they might not need to be on the LMS constantly. They might download everything once and work independently. I kind of wish I could be that person, but my anxiety kept pulling me back to check the LMS even when it wasn't productive.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and time spent, which says something about presence. But they can't show the quality of that presence or what's happening in someone's head. They can't capture offline studying, emotional states, or whether someone is actually learning versus just going through motions. They show the shadow of engagement, not engagement itself.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just showing up. It means your mind is present, you're asking questions, you're trying to understand rather than just complete tasks. For me, real engagement happened in moments when my anxiety quieted enough to let curiosity in. Those moments were rare but meaningful.
Q24: The LMS can't show my internal struggle or how hard I was fighting just to stay enrolled. It can't show the peer support conversations that kept me going, or the tears of frustration, or the offline hours spent trying to understand concepts. It can't show that my high activity was often anxiety-driven rather than learning-driven. It sees my actions but not my battle.
